title: PIP5K-Ras bistability initiates plasma membrane symmetry breaking to regulate cell polarity and migration
text:
PIP5K-Ras bistability initiates plasma membrane symmetry breaking 
1 
to regulate cell polarity and migration 
2 
Yu Deng1,2, Tatsat Banerjee1,2,6, Dhiman Sankar Pal1,6, Parijat Banerjee3, Huiwang 
3 
Zhan1, Jane Borleis1, Pablo A. Igleias1,4, Peter N. Devreotes1,5* 
4 
1Department of Cell Biology and Center for Cell Dynamics, School of Medicine, Johns 
5 
Hopkins University, Baltimore, MD, USA. 
6 
2Department of Chemical and Biomolecular Engineering, Whiting School of 
7 
Engineering, Johns Hopkins University, Baltimore, MD, USA. 
8 
3Department of Physics & Astronomy, Johns Hopkins University, Baltimore, MD, USA. 
9 
4Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, Whiting School of Engineering, 
10 
Johns Hopkins University, Baltimore, MD, USA.

12 
Baltimore, MD, USA. 
13 
6These authors contributed equally to this work 
14 
*Email for correspondence: pnd@jhmi.edu 
15 
 
16 
Abstract 
17 
Symmetry breaking, polarity establishment, and spontaneous cell protrusion 
18 
formation are fundamental but poorly explained cell behaviors. Here, we demonstrate that 
19 
a biochemical network, where the mutually inhibitory localization of PIP5K and Ras 
20 
activities plays a central role, governs these processes. First, in resting cells devoid of 
21 
cytoskeletal activity, PIP5K is uniformly elevated on the plasma membrane, while Ras 
22 
activity remains minimal. Symmetry is broken by spontaneous local displacements of 
23 
PIP5K, coupled with simultaneous activations of Ras and downstream signaling events, 
24 
including PI3K activation. Second, knockout of PIP5K dramatically increases both the 
25 
incidence and size of Ras-PI3K activation patches, accompanied by branched F-actin 
26 
assembly. This leads to enhanced cortical wave formation, increased protrusive activity, 
27 
and a shift in migration mode. Third, high inducible overexpression of PIP5K virtually 
28 
eliminates Ras-PI3K signaling, cytoskeletal activity, and cell migration, while acute 
29 
recruitment of cytosolic PIP5K to the membrane induces contraction and blebs in cancer 
30 
cells. These arrested phenotypes are reversed by reducing myosin II activity, indicating 
31 
myosin’s involvement in the PIP5K-Ras-centered regulatory network. Remarkably, low 
32 
inducible overexpression of PIP5K unexpectedly facilitates polarity establishment, 
33 
highlighting PIP5K as a highly sensitive master regulator of these processes. Simulations 
34 
of a computational model combining an excitable system, cytoskeletal loops, and dynamic 
35 
partitioning of PIP5K recreates the experimental observations. Taken together, our results 
36 
reveal that a bistable, mutually exclusive localization of PIP5K and active Ras on the 
37 
plasma membrane triggers the initial symmetry breaking. Coupled actomyosin reduction 
38 
and increased actin polymerization lead to intermittently extended protrusions and, with 
39 
.

40 
versus Ras steepen, raising the threshold of the networks at the rear and lowering it at 
41 
the front to generate polarity for cell migration.  
42 
Introduction 
43 
The diverse morphological behaviors displayed by cells all depend on the ability to 
44 
achieve an asymmetric form spontaneously or in response to an external or internal cue 
45 
[1-6]. During migration, for example, a cell must generate and maintain a distinct front 
46 
and back [7-10]. Signaling and cytoskeletal components and activities self-organize 
47 
precisely into specific front or back regions of the cortex/plasma membrane [2, 6, 11-20]. 
48 
This complementary organization is conserved across various processes, including 
49 
macropinocytosis, phagocytosis, cytokinesis, and apical-basal polarity in epithelial cells 
50 
[21-25]. Our studies focus on the role of PIP5K in regulating this elegant spatiotemporal 
51 
organization during migration. Still, the insights gained should apply broadly to symmetry-
52 
breaking processes. 
53 
Symmetry-breaking is a critical initial step, but further events must follow to achieve 
54 
effective cell migration [6, 21, 26-33].  In human leukocytes and epithelial cells, as well as 
55 
Dictyostelium amoebae, protrusions underlying cell movement are driven by 
56 
spontaneously triggered waves of Ras, PI3K, which couple to cytoskeletal events at the 
57 
front, while PTEN, RhoA, and myosin II assembly retreat from these active areas [22, 26, 
58 
34-42].  Additional important aspects of migration are directional sensing and polarity, 
59 
where cells consistently display the same front-back complementary relationship of all 
60 
these molecular events [36]. Uncovering the relationship that links the downstream 
61 
processes to the initial symmetry-breaking events would provide a deep understanding 
62 
of the basis of dynamic cellular morphology.   
63 
The initial symmetry-breaking event in migration, which can occur even in the 
64 
absence of cytoskeletal activities, is the spontaneous local activation of Ras [35, 43-45]; 
65 
however, the mechanism that controls Ras activation at specific membrane regions 
66 
remains obscure. Whatever the mechanism, it must amplify the signal locally and 
67 
simultaneously restrict it to prevent the activation elsewhere. Recent studies show that 
68 
PIP2 levels are depleted on the spontaneous protrusions [19]. Globally lowering PI(4,5)P2 
69 
activates Ras and increases the size of protrusions in Dictyostelium [46], and causes 
70 
spreading in human cells [34]. Reasoning that PIP5K could be the central regulator of 
71 
PIP2 production [47-50], we focused on the roles of this enzyme in fine-tuning Ras 
72 
activation. 
73 
Here, we report remarkably conserved localizations and outsized roles of PIP5Ks 
74 
in cell migration in amoebae, neutrophils, macrophages, and cancer cells and delineate 
75 
the function of these enzymes in cellular behavior.  A central finding is a bistable, mutually 
76 
exclusive localization of PIP5Ks and active Ras on the plasma membrane, leading to 
77 
symmetry-breaking and locally regulated signal transduction network activities. These 
78 
activities are coupled to and amplified by interactions and feedback from the cytoskeleton. 
79 
The dramatic effects of deletion or overexpression of the enzymes on protrusions, cell 
80 
migration, and polarity demonstrate the significance of the spatial-temporal regulation of 
81 
.

82 
symmetry-breaking, cell polarity, and migration.  
83 
Results 
84 
Lowering PIP2 increases signal transduction, cytoskeletal, and protrusive 
85 
activities 
86 
The inconsistency of a report that PIP5K had little effect on random cell migration 
87 
with our previous reports of strong phenotypes caused by lowering PI(4,5)P2, prompted 
88 
us to reexamine this issue [51]. More detailed observation of pi5k- (piki-) cells revealed a 
89 
heterogeneous spectrum of migratory patterns (Figure 1a-h). While a minority of cells 
90 
retained wild-type amoeboid behavior, most exhibited fan-shaped, keratocyte-like 
91 
movements or oscillatory spreading/contracting movements, consistent with the 
92 
previously reported phenotype induced by abrupt reduction of PI(4,5)P2 in wild-type cells 
93 
[46]. These observations were supported by color-coded temporal overlay profiles (Figure 
94 
1a-c). Tracking experiments showed that fan-shaped cells migrate rapidly, with speeds 
95 
reaching 16.9 ± 2.2 μm min−1 (Figure 1e, 1g), in contrast to the 6.8 ± 2.8 μm min−1 
96 
observed in WT cells (Figure 1d, 1g). Since the spreading phases of the oscillatory cells 
97 
were asymmetric, these cells also displayed increased mobility, moving at a rate of around 
98 
11.2 ± 3 μm min−1 (Figure 1f-g). Quantification of cell area of the entire population (Figure 
99 
1h) revealed an overall 4-fold increase, primarily due to flattening, although some of the 
100 
increase was due to a multinuclear phenotype of pi5k- cells (Figure S1c-d). These defects 
101 
in migration probably explain the inability of pi5k- cells to aggregate and form multicellular 
102 
structures in a timely fashion (Figure S1e).  Most of these studies were carried out on the 
103 
original pi5k- line generated by homologous recombination [51].  To validate that the 
104 
phenotypes were attributable to loss of PI5K activity, we used CRISPR-mediated 
105 
disruption of PIP5K to create an independent loss-of-function cell line. These cells 
106 
displayed a consistent heterogeneous phenotype, primarily consisting of fan-shaped and 
107 
oscillatory cell populations, with cells more spread than wild-type cells (Figure S1a-b).  
108 
To further understand the basis of these phenotypes, we examined a series of 
109 
signal transduction and cytoskeletal activities using biosensors. As expected, PH-PLCδ 
110 
was mainly found on the membrane of WT cells, suggesting a significant level of PI(4,5)P2 
111 
[19], but there was no apparent membrane association of the biosensor in most of the 
112 
pi5k- cells (Figure 1i, 1k, Video S1). Cell boundary-to-cytosol quantification showed an 
113 
apparent 78% decrease of PH-PLCδ on the membrane in pi5k- (Figure 1k). Curiously, a 
114 
few outlier cells showed a bright signal comparable to WT cells (Figure 1k); these cells 
115 
are under investigation. Next, we examined the spatial distribution of Ras activity using a 
116 
Ras binding domain (RBD) biosensor in WT and pi5k- cells. Compared with WT cells, 
117 
which have characteristic patches at the cell protrusions, pi5k- cells have much broader 
118 
RBD patches, nearly a 2.9-fold increase (Figure 1j, 1l, Video S2). These observations 
119 
were supported by membrane kymograph analyses (Figure 1m-n). Similar results were 
120 
observed using the PHcrac biosensor to examine PIP3 accumulation between pi5k- cells 
121 
and WT cells, with a 2.1-fold increase in pi5k- cells (Figure S2a, 2c, Video S2). LimE, a 
122 
biosensor reflecting newly formed F-actin, also displayed much broader patches in pi5k- 
123 
cells, with a 3-fold increase in pi5k- cells (Figure S2b, 2d, Video S2). The dynamic 
124 
.

behavior of cytoskeletal activities was captured in membrane kymographs (Figure S2h-
125 
i), which also revealed that the signaling activities oscillate in oscillatory cells (Figure S1f-
126 
g). When cells spread, the signaling activities increase, while they disappear upon cell 
127 
shrinking. The three biosensors examined are typically associated with the active, “front-
128 
state” of the cell, and the elevated levels suggest that pi5k- cells are vastly more activated 
129 
than wild-type cells.    
130 
Next, we examined signal transduction and cytoskeletal activities typically 
131 
associated with the “back-state” of cells. CynA is a biosensor that reports the level of 
132 
PI(3,4)P2 [52].  Strikingly, there generally was no detectable signal from CynA biosensor 
133 
in pi5k- cells.  This reduction is indirect since PI(3,4)P2 is not a product of PI5K.  (Figure 
134 
S2f, Video S1). However, in oscillatory cells, CynA did display a transient patch when cells 
135 
contracted, and this activity disappeared upon cell spreading (Figure S1h), indicating that 
136 
signaling activities at the cell back can also oscillate.  Similar results were observed when 
137 
we examined cytoskeletal activities at the cell back with Myosin II (Figure S2e, Video S1). 
138 
These results suggested that cell activities are low at the cell back. 
139 
We also observed the same biosensors within ventral waves in the pi5k- cells.  As 
140 
explained above, cellular protrusions consist of spontaneously initiated waves of signal 
141 
transduction and cytoskeletal activities. The waves are more conveniently observed along 
142 
the basal surface than in confocal slices.  On the ventral surface, biosensors for Ras and 
143 
PI3K activity and F-actin appear as broad propagating regions, while “back-state” 
144 
sensors, CynA and myosin II, leave these zones, creating traveling “shadow waves.”  As 
145 
shown in Figure S2n-o, ventral waves are not apparent in single WT cells and are typically 
146 
visualized in electro-fused giant cells. However, they are readily apparent without 
147 
electrofusion in the flat, multinucleated pi5k- cells [41, 46, 53-55] (Figure S2j-k, Video S3).  
148 
Since the phenotypes of pi5k- cells were similar to those induced by reduction of 
149 
PI(4,5)P2 in Dictyostelium cells [46], we used recruitment of Inp54p to extend our studies 
150 
to differentiated HL-60 neutrophils and macrophages. We developed a recruitable Inp54p 
151 
by fusing it with CRY2PHR-mCherry, which enabled light-induced association with 
152 
membrane anchor CIBN-CAAX (Figure 1r). Globally recruiting Inp54p to the plasma 
153 
membrane induced fan-shaped cells and increased F-actin activities at the protrusions 
154 
reported by the LifeAct biosensor in both differentiated HL-60 neutrophils (Figure 1o, 
155 
Video S4) and macrophages (Figure S3a, Video S4). Recruiting an empty vector did not 
156 
cause a cell shape change (Figure S3e, Video S4) [44]. These observations were 
157 
supported by membrane kymograph analyses (Figure S3j, S3l) and color-coded temporal 
158 
overlay profiles (Figure S3k, S3m). Across the population, upon Inp54p recruitment, 
159 
differentiated HL-60 neutrophils and macrophages induced a 1.43-fold and 1.5-fold 
160 
increase in the cell area and aspect ratio, respectively (Figure 1p, Figure S3b). Cell 
161 
migration speed also increased from 4.0 ± 1.4 μm min−1 (488 nm OFF) to 6.3 ± 
162 
2.2 μm min−1 (488 nm ON) for differentiated HL-60 neutrophils (Figure 1q, S3f-g) and 4.0 
163 
± 1.4 μm min−1 (488 nm OFF) to 6.3 ± 2.2 μm min−1 (488 nm ON) for differentiated HL-60 
164 
macrophages (Figure S3c, S3h-i). Although cells became fan-shaped with one broad 
165 
lamellipodia structure at the cell front, cell polarity quantified by aspect ratio did not 
166 
change significantly (Figure S2g, S3d).  
167

In summary, lowering PI(4,5)P2 levels by depleting PIP5K or recruiting Inp54p has 
168 
profound effects on the overall state of cells, generally causing Dictyostelium cells, 
169 
neutrophils, and macrophages to spread with broader protrusions and increased speed 
170 
(Figure 1s). Increased “front-state” and reduced “back-state” signal transduction and 
171 
cytoskeletal activities all suggest that cells are highly activated. These results imply that 
172 
PI5K, by regulating its product, PI(4,5)P2, is a key negative regulator of cell behavior.  
173 
PIP5Ks localize to back-state regions of the membrane 
174 
To investigate the localization of PIP5K and whether it could shed light the 
175 
decreases in PI(4,5)P2 at protrusions [19], we co-expressed PIP5K with a typical front-
176 
state biosensor PHcrac in Dictyostelium cells. The distribution of PIP5K was 
177 
complementary to PHcrac and was depleted from protrusions in migrating single cells 
178 
(Figure 2a, Video S5). The back-state localization of PIP5Ks was conserved in HL60-
179 
differentiated neutrophils. Both PIP5K1B and PIP5K1C isomers displayed a higher signal 
180 
on the back-state of the membrane in neutrophils (Figure 2b-c, Video S5) during random 
181 
migration, which was consistent with previously reported PIP5K1B and PIP5K1C back 
182 
localization during chemotaxis to N-formyl-met-leu-phe (fMLP) [49, 56].  
183 
We then examined ventral wave activities of the same cell lines in Figure 2a and 
184 
found, consistently, that PI5K was depleted in the front-state regions marked by high PIP3 
185 
and high Ras (Figure 2d, S4a, Video S6). The complementary pattern of PIP5K and PIP3 
186 
or PIP5K and Ras was maintained dynamically as the waves traveled across the basal 
187 
surface of the cells.  Line scans showed about 80% less PIP5K in the front-state regions 
188 
compared with the back-state regions in Dictyostelium cells. Next, we co-expressed 
189 
PIP5K and PI(4,5)P2 biosensor PH-PLCδ and observed ventral wave activities. PIP5K 
190 
and PH-PLCδ travel together as “shadow” waves; they co-localize at the back-state 
191 
regions of the membrane (Figure 2e, Video S6). Line scans showed that PIP5K and PH-
192 
PLCδ are largely co-localized, suggesting that the redistribution of PIP5K is the basis 
193 
PI(4,5)P2 is localized at the back-state of the membrane. Reciprocal patterns of PIP5K1B 
194 
and LifeAct were observed during frustrated phagocytosis in HL-60-differentiated 
195 
macrophages (Figure 2g, Video S6) and in RAW 264.7 macrophages, as previously 
196 
reported [54]. These results suggest that the localization of PIP5K to the rear and back-
197 
state of the cells is a highly conserved phenomenon.  
198 
Previous studies have shown that signal transduction events can be triggered, and 
199 
the membrane can be spontaneously segregated into front- and back-states in the 
200 
absence of F-actin [40, 46, 57-59]. To test whether the spatiotemporal separation of 
201 
PIP5K depends on the existence of actin barrier between front and back states, we 
202 
treated Dictyostelium cells with Latrunculin A. We observed that on the ventral surface of 
203 
these cytoskeleton-impaired cells, the asymmetric waves of PI5K can propagate, 
204 
maintaining consistent complementarity to Ras-rich domains (Figure 2f, Videos S6). 
205 
Furthermore, the data in Dictyostelium indicates that the localization of PIP5K is 
206 
independent of the cytoskeletal network.  
207

208 
front-state biosensors, such as PHcrac, transiently translocate to the membrane while 
209 
back-state proteins, such as PTEN, dissociate [60-64]. We therefore anticipated that,

440

441 
eliminate signal transduction and cytoskeletal activities and cell migration. We 
442

443

444 
Myosin II would counteract PIP5K expression and restore migration. To test this, we co-
445

446 
shown to abruptly decrease Myosin II bipolar thick filament assembly [70] (Figure 6a). In 
447 
PIP5K-expressing “rounded” cells, global recruitment of MHCKC to the plasma 
448 
membrane caused cells to polarize and migrate faster (Figure 6b, Video S24). These 
449 
observations were illustrated by color-coded temporal overlay profiles and line scans 
450 
(Figure 6c, S15a-b).  Across the population, upon MHCKC recruitment, migration speed 
451 
increased from 1.6 ± 0.6 μm min−1 to 5.4 ± 0.9 μm min−1 (Figure 6h, S15c-d). The aspect 
452 
ratio also increased by 1.23-fold (Figure 6i). A heat map of individual cell migration speed 
453 
at each time illustrates the increase in migration speed after MHCKC recruitment, which 
454 
took about 10 mins to establish (Figure 6d).

456 
ventral surface of fused giant cells. As shown in Figure 6e and Video S25, in this example, 
457

458

459

460

461

462 
laboratory demonstrating negative feedback from Myosin II to Ras [70].

The stochastic, reaction-diffusion model recreates experimental observations  
499 
We next sought a simple mathematical model that could account for the extensive 
500 
observations of the effects loss- and gain-of-function of PIP5Ks on cell migration and 
501 
polarity and explain the observed symmetry breaking in the absence of cytoskeletal 
502 
activity (Methods). The previously reported core of the model included interactions 
503 
between Ras, PIP2, and PKBA, which gave rise to excitable behavior [36, 46]. Based on 
504 
our new findings, we adjusted the inhibitory interaction from Ras to PIP2 to act through 
505 
PIP5K. This, together with the negative regulation of Ras by PI(4,5)P2, closed a double-
506 
negative feedback loop, functionally equivalent to a positive feedback loop as in the 
507 
original core model (Fig. 7A). However, based on the new findings, we made a critical 
508 
change by implementing this loop with a dynamic partitioning scheme, where PIP5K 
509 
remained on the membrane and was pushed away from regions of high Ras ([18]; 
510 
Methods). We included two further feedback loops representing the interactions of the 
511 
cytoskeleton with Ras activity, which were supported by an independently published study 
512 
as well as the current findings. The first involved the positive contribution of actin to Ras 
513

514 
regulated by PI(4,5)P2, also in a double-negative feedback motif [70]. Simulations of this 
515 
system on a one-dimensional periodic domain, representative of the cell boundary, gave 
516 
rise to long-lived periods of localized, elevated Ras activity (Fig. 7B). Kymographs of the 
517 
other model components showed that most signaling events either colocalized with Ras 
518 
(e.g., Actin and PKBA) or formed a corresponding “shadow wave” where the signaling

Discussion 
535 
Our studies reveal that spatiotemporal regulation of PIP5K on the membrane is a 
536 
primary determinant of polarity, protrusion formation, and resulting mode of migration 
537 
(Figure 8). The phenotypes we observed are consistent with the kymographs generated 
538 
in the computational simulations. In the absence of cytoskeletal activities (Figure 8a), cells 
539 
are largely in a “back” state where PIP5K is elevated and Ras activity is low. Local 
540 
spontaneous depletions of PIP5K and coordinated activations of Ras build up, breaking 
541 
symmetry (Figure 8b). Coupled myosin reduction and increased actin polymerization lead 
542 
to intermittently extended protrusions of relatively defined size and cell polarity (Figure 8c 
543 
and S17). When PIP5K is absent in pi5k- cells, Ras is highly activated and signal 
544 
transduction events spread across most of the cortex. Myosin is depleted, actin is 
545 
polymerized, protrusions are wide, and cells switch their migratory behaviors from 
546 
amoeboid to fan-shaped and oscillatory phenotypes (Figure 8d and S17). When PIP5K 
547 
is slightly overexpressed in WT, signal transduction activities and protrusions are curtailed 
548 
and cells, can polarize and migrate more rapidly (Figure 8e and S17). When PIP5K is 
549 
increased further, Ras, PI3K, and F-actin activities are confined to small patches, or 
550 
extinguished, and the entire cortex is shifted toward the back state. Protrusions become 
551 
smaller and spikier, or disappear entirely and cells stop migration. (Figure 8f and S17). In 
552 
summary, different phenotypes can be explained as lying along a spectrum from low-to-
553 
high PIP5K expression levels, which are inversely related to high-to-low signal 
554 
transduction network activities (Figure S17).   
555 
As indicated in Figure 8, our results suggested that PIP5K is a negative regulator 
556 
of signal transduction and cytoskeletal activities, which was not previously recognized. 
557 
We propose that the chemotaxis defects previously reported in pi5k- cells can be traced 
558 
to removal of the normal PIP5K-mediated constraint on Ras, leading to elevated, 
559 
unregulated signal transduction and cytoskeletal activities which interfere with the cell’s 
560 
ability to selectively direct protrusions towards chemoattractants. We found that although 
561 
the overall speed of the pi5k- cells on average does not decrease, the actual behaviors 
562 
of the cells as fans and oscillators are very defective, which was overlooked in the 
563

564 
recruiting Inp54p induced more protrusions, fan-shaped movement, and increased actin 
565 
polymerization, consistent with the phenotype of the pi5k- cells and previous results in 
566 
Dictyostelium [46]. Furthermore, previous reports that a dominant negative PIP5K1C 
567 
interferes with the uropod in neutrophils and disrupts chemotaxis may also have been 
568 
related to the loss of PIP5K function, leading to excessive Ras and associated protrusive 
569 
activity [56]. The effects of PIP5K overexpression or recruitment in Dictyostelium, 
570 
neutrophils, macrophages, and cancer-derived MDA-MB-231 cells provided further 
571 
evidence that the enzyme is a negative regulator: 1) Cell protrusions and migration speed 
572 
are severely suppressed; 2) Ras, PI3K, and F-actin activities are strongly inhibited. 3) 
573 
The dose-response curves for Ras and PI3K activation by chemoattractant are shifted to 
574 
higher concentrations. The effects of overexpression of PIP5K could be due to elevating 
575 
PIP2 levels or preventing it from going down, as it and other back localized components 
576 
normally do as protrusions form. 
577 
PIP5K plays a crucial role in symmetry breaking and polarity establishment.  As 
578 
migrating Dictyostelium cells and human neutrophils migrate, they occasionally shift 
579 
between more circular and polarized profiles whereupon PIP5K shifts between a uniform 
580 
localization and a back-to-front gradient. Since the enzyme is tightly associated with the 
581 
membrane (confirmed in centrifugation assay), it must diffuse along the membrane to the 
582 
back [18]. Protrusions are formed at the nascent front as PIP5K moves away from a local 
583 
region.  As it does not shuttle to the cytosol, PIP5K leaving the front accumulates at the 
584 
rear, suppressing Ras, PI3K, and branched actin polymerization activities at the back and 
585 
promoting a shift toward actomyosin. Thus, the spatiotemporal relocalization of PIP5K 
586 
provides a way to activate the cell front and suppress the rear simultaneously, an elegant 
587 
mechanism for symmetry breaking and self-organizing polarity. The regulation of signal 
588 
transduction by PIP5K can occur without cytoskeletal activities in the presence of 
589 
latrunculin, but there are significant interactions with actomyosin.  In untreated cells, 
590 
feedback from actomyosin is an essential part of the suppressive mechanism at the back 
591 
since inhibiting myosin II can overcome the PIP5K-mediated suppression and reactivate 
592 
the signal transduction activities.  
593 
Various models have been put forth suggesting that cell polarity arises from the 
594 
competition of local positive and global negative feedback loops. Whereas positive 
595 
feedback helps to amplify initial cellular asymmetries, negative feedback prevents other 
596 
sites from developing, ensuring that the cell has a singular region of high activity, thus 
597 
enabling persistent directional motion.  Most of these models suggest that the positive 
598 
feedback loop incorporates actin-mediated activation of various signaling molecules [81, 
599 
82]. The origin of the negative feedback is less clear, though mechanisms by which 
600 
membrane or cortical tension inhibit protrusions have been suggested [17, 29, 83]. 
601 
Computational models of these complementary motifs generate simulations of cell motility 
602 
that closely resemble the shape and tracks of real cells [84, 85].  The dynamic partitioning 
603 
of PIP5K reported here suggests an alternative actin-independent mechanism by which 
604 
the same molecule can provide both localized positive feedback and global negative 
605 
feedback. At the regions of high Ras activity, the removal of PIP5K lowers the levels of 
606 
the Ras-inhibiting PIP2.  This double-negative feedback loop provides localized positive 
607 
feedback.  Additionally, the dispersal of this membrane-bound PIP5K to regions away

609

610 
There are numerous reported interactions of PIP2 with various actin-binding 
611 
proteins, but as some are reported to be activators and others are inhibitors, the overall 
612 
effects of altering PIP2 in cells are challenging to understand. Some of the studies 
613 
suggest that PIP2 sequesters critical actin-binding proteins. This mechanism would align 
614 
with our proposal that PIP5K is a negative regulator: Van Rheenen et al. reported that 
615 
EGF-induces PIP2 reduction in metastatic tumor cells and promotes actin polymerization 
616 
[86]; Sengelaub et al. reported that in metastatic breast cancer cells, lowering PI(4,5)P2 
617 
levels releases cofilin to enhance actin turnover and cell migration [87]. Alternative studies 
618 
suggested that PIP2 is an activator: It can directly interact with WASP to promote Arp2/3 
619 
complex nucleation [74] and it can bind to proteins like talin, vinculin, and focal adhesion 
620 
kinases (FAK), to promote focal adhesion formation [88-93]. Our study indicates that 
621 
inhibition to Ras by PIP5K, transmitted downstream, shifts the balance from branched F-
622 
actin to actomyosin and that the inhibitory role of PIP5Ks occurs in cells that do not form 
623 
stable focal adhesions. Thus, the overall direction of the behavior in living cells favors 
624 
PIP5K as a negative regulator regardless of potential direct interactions of PI(4,5)P2 with 
625 
individual actin-binding proteins.  
626 
The counterintuitive effects of PIP5K inhibition on cell migration can be explained 
627 
by the Ras-PIP5K mutual inhibitory feedback loop [46], with additional feedback loops 
628 
from the cytoskeletal network, shown in Figure 7 and 8, but further studies are needed to 
629 
sort out the detailed mechanism. Even without the cytoskeletal network, there is a strong 
630 
reciprocal connection between Ras and PIP5K. It has been reported that Ras can directly 
631 
interact with PIP5K [94]; if that is the case, the interaction must push Ras towards the 
632 
inactive form to be consistent with our observations. It is possible that PIP5K or PIP2 
633 
interacts with Ras GAPs or competes with Ras GEFs to inhibit Ras activities. We are 
634 
currently testing these possibilities. In the presence of the cytoskeletal network, PIP2 may 
635 
regulate the interaction between the membrane and the actomyosin cortex [38]. While 
636 
raising PIP2 would increase the interaction, lowering PIP2 may loosen the linkage, and 
637 
could initiate a shift from actomyosin to branched actin. Such a mechanism would be 
638 
consistent with the model of front and back-promoting feedback loops we recently 
639 
reported [46, 70].  
640 
Our results suggest that phenotypes resulting from loss-of-function or gain-of-
641 
function alterations of PIP5K in tissue will likely depend on the initial state of the affected 
642 
cells and may be difficult to predict. Without this study, PIP5K might be considered an 
643 
oncogene since it is the substrate for producing second messengers, such as PIP3, DAG, 
644 
and IP3, which are classic tumor promoters. The depletion of PIP5K and its product PIP2 
645 
from the front of the cells is puzzling since these messengers are localized at the front. 
646 
However, even when PIP2 is lowered by as much as 90%, PIP3 is more elevated, and 
647 
when PIP5K is overexpressed, Ras and PI3K activity are severely inhibited. Based on 
648 
this, one might expect PIP5K to act like a tumor suppressor and consider activating or 
649 
increasing PIP5K as a therapeutic mechanism to suppress activated oncogenic Ras. Our 
650 
results show that a slight increase in PIP5K and a decrease in PIP3 activity can enhance 
651 
polarity, while a large rise in PIP5K and elimination in PIP3 completely shut down the 
652

a straight-line segment (using line tool) inside the cells with a line width of 8–12 pixels to 
1049 
obtain an average intensity value. The intensity values along that particular line were 
1050 
obtained in the green and red channels using the ‘Plot Profile’ option. The values were 
1051 
saved and normalized in OriginPro 9.0 (OriginLab). The intensity profiles were graphed 
1052 
and finally smoothened using the adjacent-averaging method, using proper boundary 
1053 
conditions. The plots were then normalized by dividing by the maximum value. For a 
1054 
particular line scan, the green and red profiles were smoothed and processed using the 
1055 
exact same parameters to maintain consistency. 
1056 
Membrane to cytosol ratio quantification. The cytosol of cells was first outlined with 
1057 
freehand selection tool in Fiji/ImageJ (NIH). The mean intensity of the cytosol was 
1058 
measured using analyze>>measure function. Then make band function was applied to 
1059 
the outline and proper band size was applied according to the cells. Then mean 
1060 
membrane intensity was measured using the same method. Using mean membrane to 
1061 
cytosol intensity to divide mean cytosol intensity, we can get the membrane to cytosol 
1062 
ratio for each biosensor. Each data was then plotted as box-and-whisker graphs in 
1063 
GraphPad Prism 10. 
1064 
Cell migration analysis Analysis was performed by segmenting Dictyostelium or 
1065 
neutrophil cells in Fiji/ImageJ 1.52i software. For this, image stack was thresholded using 
1066 
the ‘Threshold’ option. ‘Calculate threshold for each image’ box was unchecked, and 
1067 
range was not reset. Next, cell masks were created by size-based thresholding using the 
1068 
‘Analyzed particles’ option. To optimize binarized masks, ‘Fill holes’, ‘Dilate’, and ‘Erode’ 
1069 
were done several times. For creating temporal color-coded cell outlines, ‘Outline’ was 
1070 
applied on binarized masks, followed by ‘Temporal-Color Code’ option. Next, ‘Centroid’ 
1071 
and ‘Shape descriptors’ boxes were checked in ‘Set Measurements’ option under 
1072 
‘Analyze’ tab. This provided us with values for centroid coordinates and aspect ratio. 
1073 
Mean and SEM from replicates of aspect ratio values were determined and plotted in 
1074 
GraphPad Prism 10. The starting point for centroid values was set to zero for each track, 
1075 
and these new coordinates were plotted in Microsoft Excel to generate migration tracks. 
1076

1077

Statistics and reproducibility 
1145 
 
Statistical analyses were executed using unpaired or paired two-tailed 
1146 
nonparametric tests on GraphPad Prism 10. Results are expressed as mean ± s.d. from 
1147 
at least three independent experiments. NS, P > 0.05, *P ≤ 0.05, **P ≤ 0.01, ***P ≤ 0.001, 
1148 
***P ≤ 0.0001. Tukey’s convention was used to plot box-and-whisker plots. Statistical test 
1149 
details are indicated in the figure legends. No statistical methods were used to 
1150 
predetermine sample sizes, but our sample sizes are similar to those reported in previous 
1151 
publications4,35,79. Each representative image or image series is from n > 3 independent 
1152 
experiments. Any variations observed between treatment groups are not attributed to 
1153 
sampling bias. No data were excluded from the analyses.
